one_line_takeaway: StealthInk is a distribution-preserving multi-bit LLM watermark whose statistical stealthiness buys markedly better resistance to watermark spoofing than distortion-based schemes, at the price of needing longer text to reach comparable clean detectability, while paraphrasing robustness stays unsolved.
key_finding: StealthInk uniquely satisfies all five properties in the authors' comparison table (stealthiness, efficiency, high AUC, high bit accuracy, robustness) whereas each SOTA baseline fails at least one. At 24 bits in 200 tokens it reaches AUC 0.978, TPR@1%FPR 0.8475, bit accuracy 0.8747, PPL 7.141, and 0.01s extraction. Crucially, its stealthiness translates into the strongest spoofing resistance: forged StealthInk text on Dolly-CW is detected as watermarked with AUC 0.7265 vs. 0.8301 (MPAC) and 0.9835 (Qu et al.), i.e., only ~29% of forgeries pass at TPR@10%FPR. The trade-off is that distribution-modifying baselines (MPAC delta=2, Qu et al.) get higher clean detectability at short lengths, but at severe text-quality cost (Qu et al. PPL ~42 vs StealthInk ~7) or 4-5 orders of magnitude slower extraction (Fernandez et al.: 113s at 24 bits, estimated 45,100s at 36 bits). StealthInk closes the detectability gap given enough tokens (e.g., matches MPAC delta=2's TPR@1%FPR at 36 bits by 400 tokens).
core_contribution: StealthInk is a multi-bit, provably-stealthy (distribution-preserving) watermarking scheme for LLMs that embeds provenance metadata (e.g., userID, TimeStamp, modelID) into generated text without needing access to the model's API or prompts at detection time. Its own novel mechanism is a token-probability reweighting rule: within a pseudorandomly-permuted vocabulary, tokens in a message-dependent 'red list' interval have their probability zeroed while certain other intervals are doubled, so that the watermarked distribution equals the original distribution in expectation over the random permutation (the unbiasedness / K-shot stealthiness property, Def. 4.1-4.2, Thm 4.3-4.4). The message is split into H chunks of m bits each and pseudorandomly allocated across token positions (borrowing MPAC's position-allocation idea) to raise capacity. Detection counts red-list tokens per position (RM_pos ~ Bin(Lpos, gamma) under the null), picks the message minimizing red-token count, and computes a z-score. The paper also derives a theoretical lower bound Lmin on the number of tokens needed to detect the watermark at a fixed equal-error-rate, showing capacity is best increased by spreading unit capacity across positions and that repeated texture keys (rate p) sharply raise Lmin.
threat_model: Following Jovanovic et al. (2024): a model owner deploys an LLM with a secret watermark key sk. The attacker has only black-box (API) access to full generations, knows a watermark is present, and per Kerckhoffs' principle knows all scheme parameters EXCEPT sk. Three attack goals are considered: (1) watermark spoofing/forgery -- the attacker queries the owner's model to learn its watermarking rules and forge text that a detector will flag as watermarked (damaging the provider's reputation), using an auxiliary open model (LMatt = Mistral-7B); (2) watermark removal via editing attacks (substitution/insertion/deletion, and copy-paste mixing of non-watermarked text); and (3) paraphrasing attacks that rewrite watermarked text while preserving meaning. StealthInk's central security claim is that its stealthiness (statistical indistinguishability of watermarked vs. non-watermarked text without sk) makes spoofing much harder, because per-query history logs keep the texture-key repetition low and an attacker would need to repeat the same prompt with the same userID within ~1 second and gather ~100,000 queries to exploit any distributional difference.
limitations: The authors explicitly concede paraphrasing robustness remains a major open challenge: bit accuracy for 24 bits in 300 tokens collapses from 0.9195 (clean) to 0.6118 (Dipper) / 0.57 (PEGASUS) -- and all baselines degrade similarly, so this is a general watermarking weakness, not solved here. StealthInk also has strictly lower clean detectability and bit accuracy than the distortion-based MPAC and Qu et al. at short sequence lengths, requiring longer text to catch up. The chosen configuration favors m=1 (H bits over H chunks) because higher m per chunk sharply reduces TPR. Enhancing capacity via key enumeration causes large detection delays. The reported StealthInk PPL being LOWER than non-watermarked text is an evaluation artifact (red-list filtering removes low-probability tokens), which the authors argue converges with more/longer samples but signals the PPL comparison is not a clean like-for-like. Evaluation is limited to LLAMA2-7B and modest generation lengths.

datasets_used: C4 RealNewsLike subset (Raffel et al. 2020) -- 500 texts for text completion; 30,000 prompts for spoofing training
WMT'14 English-to-Romanian (Bojar et al. 2014) -- machine translation quality
Dolly-CW (Conover et al. 2023) -- spoofing attack prompts
MMW BookReports (Piet et al. 2023) -- spoofing attack prompts
quantitative_results: 24-bit @ 200 tokens (Table 4): StealthInk AUC 0.9780, TPR@1%FPR 0.8475, bit accuracy 0.8747, PPL 7.141, extraction 0.01s (non-watermarked PPL 8.016)
36-bit @ 200 tokens (Table 4): StealthInk AUC 0.9709, TPR@1%FPR 0.7475, bit accuracy 0.8327, PPL 7.135
48-bit @ 200 tokens (Table 4): StealthInk AUC 0.9556, TPR@1%FPR 0.6185, bit accuracy 0.7867, PPL 6.968
Abstract headline: AUC 0.98 and bit accuracy 0.92 at 24-bit message in 300 tokens
Spoofing on Dolly-CW, H=1 (Table 3): StealthInk AUC 0.7265 / TPR@10%FPR 0.29 vs MPAC 0.8301/0.60 vs Qu et al. 0.9835/0.97 (lower is better spoofing resistance)
Spoofing on MMW BookReports, H=1 (Table 3): StealthInk AUC 0.8064 vs MPAC 0.86 vs Qu et al. 0.954
MT quality 1-bit (Table 2): StealthInk BERTScore 0.6941 / BLEU 0.0776 vs non-watermarked 0.6995/0.0677; MPAC 0.6457/0.0442; Qu et al. 0.5840/0.0180
Summarization PPL (Table 2): StealthInk 7.147 (1-bit) vs non-watermarked 7.274 vs MPAC 10.51 vs Qu et al. 26.27
Copy-paste attack, 24 bits @ 300 tokens (Table 5): StealthInk bit accuracy 0.9195 (eps=0) -> 0.8319 (eps=0.3); MPAC 0.9627->0.9075; Qu et al. ~0.999
Paraphrasing attack, 24 bits @ 300 tokens (Table 6): StealthInk bit accuracy 0.9195 -> 0.6118 (Dipper) / 0.57 (PEGASUS)
Extraction time (Table 4): StealthInk & MPAC 0.01s vs Fernandez et al. 113s (24-bit), estimated 45,100s (36-bit), 1.85e9s (48-bit)
baselines_compared: MPAC (Yoo et al. 2024) -- primary distortion-based multi-bit baseline, tested at logit bias delta in {1, 1.5, 2}
Qu et al. (2024) -- cyclic-shift / green-list multi-bit watermark
Fernandez et al. (2023) 'Three bricks' -- key-enumeration multi-bit watermark

Referenced categories: distortion-free group A (Kordi Boroujeny et al. 2024; Zamir 2024); proxy/shift group B (Wang et al. 2024; Fernandez et al. 2023); Dipmark (Wu et al. 2024) and ZMH (Hu et al. 2024) as stealthy zero-bit precursors
